concepts.finding:
To overcome the second problem, that large co-occurrence does
not necessarily imply that queries are related, we propose two meth-
ods for ﬁnding recommendations, which turn out to be complemen-
tary.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
EXPERIMENTS
The goal of our experiments is two-fold: to demonstrate that our
method can successfully ﬁnd high quality query communities in real
search data, and to show that query communities increase both the
number and the quality of recommendations.
concepts.implication: Inform search ranking and retrieval optimization

concepts.finding:
An extensive series of
experiments on real search logs shows that the query communities
found by our algorithm are more interesting and unexpected than a
baseline of clustering the query-click graph.
concepts.implication: Can inform intent modeling for recommendation systems

concepts.finding:

concepts.finding:
Thus the performance of an al-
gorithm that clusters the query-click graph is upper bounded by the
relevance of a search engine, we seek to go beyond that.
concepts.implication: Applicable to user/item clustering for personalization

concepts.finding:
Recommendations: In the area of query suggestions, there is a
plethora of methods including random walks on click graphs [6, 19,
10], snippets of search results [26], query reformulation graphs [13,
4], and clustering the query-click graph [5].
concepts.implication: Applicable to user/item clustering for personalization

concepts.finding:
Finally, there is rich literature in the context of recommendation
systems, which includes content-based approaches, collaborative-
ﬁltering based approaches [27, 21, 12, 15, 1], and hybrid approaches
[28].
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
Next, we propose a method that ﬁnds recommendations through a
novel way of viewing connections between queries as ‘explanations’
rather than as co-occurrence counts.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
For the recommendation problem, we begin by showing that an
existing query suggestion algorithm [10] is not designed to pro-
duce commercial recommendations.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
The
main reason is that users can only click on what they see, and the
clusters can only be as good as the search engine.
concepts.implication: Applicable to user/item clustering for personalization

concepts.finding:
What we observe is that longer-range commer-
cial recommendations are rare, in fact in many cases there does not
seem to be a path from a search query to a commercial query in the
query-click graph.
concepts.implication: Directly applicable to CTR/CVR prediction in advertising systems

concepts.finding:
CONCLUSIONS & FUTURE WORK
We presented an approach to forecast future commercial intent
of search users, thereby enabling product recommendation early in
the search process.
concepts.implication: Inform search ranking and retrieval optimization

concepts.finding:
In addition, one may consider the problem of assembling a diver-
siﬁed portfolio of product recommendations for each query, so as to
maximize the probability that a user clicks on at least one, i.e., min-
imize the probability the portfolio defaults.
concepts.implication: Directly applicable to CTR/CVR prediction in advertising systems

Context-aware
query suggestion by mining click-through and session data.

concepts.finding:
We propose two complemen-
tary approaches for inferring recommendations that utilize query
communities in order to magnify the recommendation signal be-
yond what an individual query can provide.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
Also, whereas existing
query suggestion algorithms are not designed for making commer-
cial recommendations, we show that our algorithms do succeed in
forecasting commercial intent.
